question_text: When shares are issued at a price equal to their face value, they are said to be issued:
explanation: Shares issued at a price equal to face value are issued 'at par'. For example, a share with face value ₹10 issued at ₹10 is issued at par. No premium or discount is involved.
subject: accountancy

options.text: At a premium
At par
At a discount
For consideration other than cash

elimination_hints.hint: Premium means issue price > face value. 'At par' means issue price = face value.
Discount means issue price < face value. 'At par' means equal.
Issue for consideration other than cash means paying with assets, not money. This is unrelated to par value.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing mode of payment with pricing.

question_text: When shares are issued at ₹12 each (face value ₹10), the extra ₹2 is called:
explanation: The excess of issue price over face value is called 'Securities Premium'. Here, face value = ₹10, issue price = ₹12, so premium = ₹2 per share. This premium is credited to Securities Premium Reserve Account.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Securities Premium
Capital Reserve
Goodwill
Share Discount

elimination_hints.hint: Capital Reserve arises from non-trading profits (e.g., profit on reissue of forfeited shares). Premium on issue is specifically 'Securities Premium'.
Goodwill is an intangible asset. The premium on share issue is a reserve, not an asset.
Discount means LESS than face value. Here the issue is at MORE than face value = premium.

question_text: Securities Premium Reserve can be used for all of the following EXCEPT:
explanation: Under Section 52 of the Companies Act, Securities Premium can only be used for: (1) issuing fully paid bonus shares, (2) writing off preliminary expenses, (3) writing off commission/discount on issue of shares or debentures, (4) buying back shares, (5) writing off premium on redemption of debentures/preference shares. It CANNOT be used to pay dividends.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Issuing fully paid bonus shares
Writing off preliminary expenses
Writing off discount on issue of shares
Payment of dividends to shareholders

elimination_hints.hint: Issuing bonus shares is a permitted use under Section 52. Securities premium CAN be used for this.
Writing off preliminary expenses is explicitly permitted under Section 52.
Writing off share/debenture issue discount is a permitted use under Section 52.

question_text: A company issues 10,000 shares of ₹10 each at ₹13 per share, payable as: Application ₹4, Allotment ₹5 (including premium ₹3), First & Final Call ₹4. The entry on allotment is:
explanation: On allotment, amount due = ₹5 per share (₹2 towards face value + ₹3 premium). Entry: Share Allotment A/c Dr. ₹50,000 (10,000 × 5), To Share Capital A/c ₹20,000 (10,000 × 2), To Securities Premium Reserve A/c ₹30,000 (10,000 × 3).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Share Allotment Dr. ₹50,000 → To Share Capital ₹50,000
Share Allotment Dr. ₹50,000 → To Share Capital ₹20,000, To Securities Premium ₹30,000
Share Allotment Dr. ₹20,000 → To Share Capital ₹20,000
Bank Dr. ₹50,000 → To Share Allotment ₹50,000

elimination_hints.hint: This credits the entire ₹50,000 to Share Capital. But ₹30,000 is premium — it goes to Securities Premium Reserve.
₹20,000 is only the capital portion. The allotment is for ₹50,000 total (₹20,000 capital + ₹30,000 premium).
This is the cash receipt entry (Bank Dr., Allotment Cr.), not the allotment due entry. Two separate entries are needed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the 'amount due' entry with the 'amount received' entry.

question_text: The correct sequence of collecting money from shareholders on share issue is:
explanation: Money is collected in stages: (1) Application — amount paid when applying for shares, (2) Allotment — amount due when shares are allotted, (3) Call(s) — remaining amount called in one or more instalments. This is the standard sequence for share issue.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Application → Allotment → Call(s)
Allotment → Application → Call(s)
Call(s) → Application → Allotment
Application → Call(s) → Allotment

elimination_hints.hint: Application comes BEFORE allotment. You apply first, then the company allots shares.
Calls are made AFTER allotment, not before application. The sequence starts with application.
Allotment comes before calls. Shares must be allotted before any call can be made.

question_text: Under the Companies Act, 2013, shares can be issued at a discount:
explanation: Under the Companies Act, 2013, shares CANNOT be issued at a discount. Section 53 prohibits the issue of shares at a discount (except sweat equity shares under Section 54). The erstwhile Section 79 of the Companies Act, 1956 that permitted discount issue has been removed.
subject: accountancy

options.text: If the company has been in existence for at least 1 year
If approved by SEBI
If the discount does not exceed 10% of face value
Shares cannot be issued at discount under the 2013 Act (except sweat equity)

elimination_hints.hint: This was a condition under the old Companies Act, 1956. The 2013 Act has completely prohibited discount issue.
SEBI regulates listed companies' securities market activities but doesn't grant discount permissions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking SEBI can override company law.

elimination_hints.hint: The 10% limit was under the old Act. The 2013 Act has no discount provision (except sweat equity).

question_text: When shares are issued for consideration other than cash (e.g., to a vendor for purchase of assets), the entry is:
explanation: When shares are issued to a vendor for purchase of assets: the vendor (supplier of assets) is a creditor. The entry settles this liability. Entry: Vendor's A/c Dr. (amount owed), To Share Capital A/c (face value), To Securities Premium A/c (if at premium).
subtopic: Issue for consideration other than cash
subject: accountancy

options.text: Bank A/c Dr. → To Share Capital A/c
Sundry Assets A/c Dr. → To Vendor A/c
Vendor A/c Dr. → To Share Capital A/c (and Securities Premium A/c if applicable)
Share Capital A/c Dr. → To Vendor A/c

elimination_hints.hint: No cash (Bank) is involved. The consideration is assets, not money.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming all share issues involve cash.

elimination_hints.hint: This is the entry for PURCHASING assets from the vendor. The share issue entry settles the vendor's account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing asset purchase entry with share issue entry.

elimination_hints.hint: Share Capital is credited (increased) when shares are issued, not debited.

question_text: A company issued 5,000 shares of ₹10 each at ₹15 per share. Application ₹5, Allotment ₹5 (incl. premium ₹3), First Call ₹3, Final Call ₹2. Mr. X, holding 200 shares, did not pay the First Call. The amount of calls-in-arrears from Mr. X is:
explanation: Mr. X didn't pay First Call. First Call = ₹3 per share. Mr. X holds 200 shares. Calls-in-arrears = 200 × ₹3 = ₹600.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹400 = 200 × ₹2 (final call). But Mr. X didn't pay the FIRST call (₹3), not the final call.
₹1,000 = 200 × ₹5 (first call + final call). But only the first call is unpaid, not both.

question_text: The minimum amount payable on application for shares cannot be less than:
explanation: As per SEBI guidelines, the minimum application money on shares must be at least 5% of the nominal value (face value) of shares. However, under the Companies Act, it should not be less than 5% of the nominal value. In practice, application money is typically much higher.
subject: accountancy

options.text: 5% of the nominal value of shares
10% of the nominal value of shares
25% of the nominal value of shares
50% of the nominal value of shares

elimination_hints.hint: 10% is not the statutory minimum. The minimum is 5% of the nominal value.
25% is much higher than required. The statutory minimum is only 5%.
50% is excessively high for minimum application. The rule requires only 5%.

question_text: A company issued 20,000 shares of ₹10 each at ₹12, payable as: Application ₹3, Allotment ₹5 (incl. premium ₹2), First & Final Call ₹4. All money received. Total amount credited to Securities Premium Reserve is:
explanation: Premium = ₹2 per share on 20,000 shares = ₹40,000. The premium is collected at the allotment stage. Total Securities Premium = 20,000 × ₹2 = ₹40,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹20,000 = 20,000 × ₹1. But the premium is ₹2 per share, not ₹1.
₹2,40,000 = 20,000 × ₹12 (total issue price). Premium is only the excess over face value.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using total issue price as premium.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹60,000 = 20,000 × ₹3. ₹3 is the application money, not the premium.

question_text: Securities Premium Reserve is a capital reserve and can be distributed as dividend.
explanation: FALSE. Securities Premium Reserve is NOT distributable as dividend. Under Section 52, it can only be used for specific purposes: issuing bonus shares, writing off preliminary expenses, writing off discount on issue, buying back shares, or writing off premium payable on redemption.
subject: accountancy

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: Securities Premium has restricted uses under Section 52. Dividend payment is NOT one of them.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating Securities Premium as freely distributable.

question_text: Premium on shares can be collected at any stage — application, allotment, or call.
explanation: TRUE. Securities premium can be collected at any stage of share issue — application, allotment, or call. The company decides at which stage to collect it as per the terms of issue. Most commonly, it is collected at allotment.
subject: accountancy

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: There's no legal restriction on when premium is collected. The company's terms of issue decide the stage.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking premium can only be collected at allotment.

question_text: When shares are issued at a price higher than their face value, the excess amount is credited to _______ Account.
explanation: The excess of issue price over face value is called Securities Premium. It is credited to Securities Premium Reserve Account. This account has restricted uses under Section 52 of the Companies Act, 2013.
subject: accountancy

question_text: Under Section 53 of the Companies Act, 2013, a company cannot issue shares at a _______ except as sweat equity shares.
explanation: Section 53 prohibits issue of shares at a DISCOUNT. The only exception is sweat equity shares (under Section 54) which may be issued at a discount to employees/directors for their intellectual property rights or value addition.
subject: accountancy

question_text:
Assertion (A): Share Application Account is a personal account.
Reason (R): Share Application Account represents the applicants (persons) who have applied for shares.

Choose the correct option:
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Share Application Account represents the applicants — a group of persons. Since it represents persons, it is classified as a personal account. It is debited when due and credited when received/transferred.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains why the account is personal — it represents persons (applicants).
R is correct — Share Application Account does represent the applicants as a group.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting that the account represents persons.

elimination_hints.hint: A is correct — Share Application Account is indeed a personal account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking it's a nominal or real account.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Securities Premium Reserve cannot be used to pay dividends.
Reason (R): Securities Premium is a capital profit that must be preserved for protecting creditors' interests.

Choose the correct option:
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Securities Premium is capital in nature (received from shareholders as part of their capital contribution, not from business operations). Like capital, it must be maintained to protect creditors. Section 52 restricts its use accordingly.
subtopic: Nature and restriction of Securities Premium
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The capital nature and creditor protection are precisely why Section 52 restricts its use — R explains A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not connecting capital nature with usage restrictions.

elimination_hints.hint: R is correct — Securities Premium IS capital in nature and the restriction IS for creditor protection.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the capital nature of premium.

elimination_hints.hint: A is correct — Section 52 explicitly does not list dividend payment as a permitted use.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking premium can be used for dividends.

question_text: A company purchased machinery worth ₹5,00,000 from a vendor and issued 40,000 equity shares of ₹10 each at a premium of ₹2.50 per share as full settlement. The journal entry for issue of shares to vendor is:
explanation: Shares issued to vendor: 40,000 × ₹10 = ₹4,00,000 (Share Capital) + 40,000 × ₹2.50 = ₹1,00,000 (Securities Premium) = ₹5,00,000 total. Entry: Vendor A/c Dr. ₹5,00,000, To Share Capital ₹4,00,000, To Securities Premium ₹1,00,000.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Vendor Dr. ₹5,00,000 → To Share Capital ₹5,00,000
Machinery Dr. ₹5,00,000 → To Share Capital ₹4,00,000, To Premium ₹1,00,000
Vendor Dr. ₹4,00,000 → To Share Capital ₹4,00,000
Vendor Dr. ₹5,00,000 → To Share Capital ₹4,00,000, To Securities Premium ₹1,00,000

elimination_hints.hint: This credits the entire ₹5,00,000 to Share Capital. But premium (₹1,00,000) must go to Securities Premium Reserve.
Machinery was already debited when purchased. This entry is for SHARE ISSUE to settle the vendor, not asset purchase.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing purchase entry with share issue entry.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹4,00,000 is only the face value. The vendor's account must be settled for the full ₹5,00,000.

question_text:
Match the following share issue terms with their entries:

| Column I (Stage) | Column II (Entry) |
|---|---|
| (i) Application received | (p) Share Allotment Dr. → To Share Capital, To Sec. Premium |
| (ii) Application money transferred | (q) Bank A/c Dr. → To Share Application A/c |
| (iii) Allotment due | (r) Share Application A/c Dr. → To Share Capital A/c |
| (iv) Allotment money received | (s) Bank A/c Dr. → To Share Allotment A/c |

Choose the correct matching:
explanation:
(i) Application received (cash in) → Bank Dr., Share Application Cr. → (q)
(ii) Application transferred to capital → Share Application Dr., Share Capital Cr. → (r)
(iii) Allotment due → Share Allotment Dr., Share Capital Cr., Sec. Premium Cr. → (p)
(iv) Allotment money received → Bank Dr., Share Allotment Cr. → (s)
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Application RECEIVED means cash comes in → Bank Dr. (q), not Application Dr. (r).
Application TRANSFERRED to capital is (r), not (p). (p) is the allotment due entry.
Application received is not Bank Dr., Share Allotment Cr. (s). Allotment hasn't happened yet at application stage.

question_text: A company issues 50,000 shares of ₹10 each at ₹10. Application ₹3, Allotment ₹3, First Call ₹2, Final Call ₹2. Mr. Y holding 500 shares paid the entire amount at the time of application. The calls-in-advance from Mr. Y at application stage is:
explanation: Application due = ₹3 per share. Mr. Y paid ₹10 per share (full amount) at application for 500 shares. Calls-in-advance = (₹10 − ₹3) × 500 = ₹7 × 500 = ₹3,500. This is the excess paid before it was due.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,500 = 500 × ₹3 = application money due. But advance = total paid − due = ₹5,000 − ₹1,500 = ₹3,500.
elimination_hints.misconception: Calculating amount due instead of advance.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹5,000 = 500 × ₹10 = total paid. But advance is only the excess (₹10 − ₹3) × 500 = ₹3,500.
₹2,000 = 500 × ₹4 (first + final call). But advance includes allotment too: ₹3 + ₹2 + ₹2 = ₹7 × 500.
elimination_hints.misconception: Counting only call amounts, not allotment.

question_text: The entry for receiving calls-in-advance is:
explanation: When a shareholder pays more than what's due (advance): Bank A/c Dr. (cash received), To Calls-in-Advance A/c (liability to the shareholder for the excess). Calls-in-advance appears as a current liability because the company owes the shareholder until the calls are made.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Bank A/c Dr. → To Calls-in-Advance A/c
Calls-in-Advance A/c Dr. → To Bank A/c
Bank A/c Dr. → To Share Capital A/c
Calls-in-Advance A/c Dr. → To Share Capital A/c

elimination_hints.hint: This debits Calls-in-Advance and credits Bank — meaning cash goes out. But cash is coming IN from the shareholder.
The advance cannot be credited to Share Capital yet — the call hasn't been made. It's a temporary liability.
No cash movement and wrong logic. The advance is received in cash and held as a liability until the call is made.
elimination_hints.misconception: Creating a non-cash entry for a cash receipt.
